title: Quiz: Electron Correlation Methods
description: Test your understanding of electron correlation and post-Hartree-Fock methods

questions.question: What is electron correlation energy?

options.text: The total energy of the electrons
The difference between exact and Hartree-Fock energies

questions.explanation: Correlation energy is defined as E_corr = E_exact - E_HF. It represents the energy lowering from the instantaneous electron-electron avoidance that Hartree-Fock's mean-field approximation misses.

questions.question: Why is the correlation energy always negative?

options.text: HF overestimates electron-electron repulsion by ignoring correlation

questions.explanation: In reality, electrons avoid each other more effectively than the HF mean-field picture suggests. This creates a 'Coulomb hole' that reduces electron-electron repulsion, making the true energy lower than HF: E_exact < E_HF.

questions.question: What distinguishes static from dynamic correlation?

options.text: Static correlation is present only in atoms
Static correlation requires multiple determinants for qualitative correctness

questions.explanation: Static (non-dynamic) correlation occurs when multiple electronic configurations are nearly degenerate and contribute significantly. Examples include stretched bonds and transition metal systems. A single Slater determinant is qualitatively wrong in these cases.

questions.question: What is the computational scaling of CCSD(T)?
questions.explanation: CCSD(T) scales as O(N⁷) because of the perturbative triples correction. This is more expensive than CCSD (O(N⁶)) but much cheaper than full CCSDT (O(N⁸)).

questions.question: Truncated CI methods (like CISD) are size-consistent.
questions.explanation: False. Truncated CI methods are NOT size-consistent. For two non-interacting fragments, E(A...B) ≠ E(A) + E(B). This is a major limitation of CISD and related methods. Coupled Cluster methods ARE size-consistent.

questions.question: Why is CCSD(T) called the 'gold standard' of quantum chemistry?

options.text: It captures ~99% of correlation energy for well-behaved systems
It works for all molecules equally well

questions.explanation: CCSD(T) typically recovers about 99% of the correlation energy for single-reference systems at a manageable O(N⁷) cost. It's the de facto standard for high-accuracy thermochemistry, although it struggles with strongly correlated systems.

questions.question: What makes MP2 popular for large molecules?

options.text: It scales as O(N⁵) and captures 85-90% of correlation

questions.explanation: MP2 offers an excellent cost/accuracy trade-off: O(N⁵) scaling (much cheaper than CC methods) while capturing 85-90% of the correlation energy. It's size-consistent and often gives good geometries.

questions.question: Which statements about quantum computing and electron correlation are true? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Quantum computers can naturally represent entangled electronic states
Full CI is the classical limit that quantum computers aim to match
VQE can find correlated ground states in polynomial time
Quantum computers have no advantage for electron correlation

questions.explanation: a) True: Qubits naturally represent entanglement. b) True: Full CI is exact within a basis but classically intractable. c) True: VQE/QPE can achieve this exponential speedup. d) False: Correlation is precisely where quantum computers have advantage.
